title: Reading and Use of English – Part 7
description: You are going to read an article about the history of spices. Choose from paragraphs A–G which fits each gap. There is one extra paragraph you do not need to use.

content.articleTitle: The Quest for Flavor: A Global Obsession
content.text: In the modern supermarket, a jar of cinnamon or black pepper is a mundane sight, costing only a few dollars. However, for most of recorded history, these aromatic substances were the most valuable commodities on Earth, more precious than gold or gemstones. The quest for spices drove the Age of Discovery, redrew the maps of the world, and sparked brutal conflicts between empires. ||(41)|| [41]This air of mystery was no accident; it was a deliberate marketing strategy[/41]. Arab merchants, who controlled the overland routes for centuries, invented elaborate myths about spice-harvesting. They claimed that cinnamon was gathered from the nests of giant birds on inaccessible cliffs, a fiction designed to protect their monopoly and justify the exorbitant prices they charged European elites. ||(42)|| [42]This shift in the global balance of power began when explorers like Vasco da Gama successfully rounded the Cape of Good Hope[/42]. By finding a direct sea route to the 'Spice Islands' of Indonesia, the Portuguese bypassed the traditional middlemen. Suddenly, the secret sources of nutmeg, cloves, and mace were exposed to the West, initiating a period of intense colonial competition. ||(43)|| [43]The Dutch East India Company eventually emerged as the dominant force in this struggle[/43]. They enforced their control with a ruthlessness that is often glossed over in history books. To keep prices high, they would burn entire plantations of spice trees on islands they did not occupy, ensuring that supply remained strictly limited. ||(44)|| [44]Beyond their use in the kitchen, spices were also essential in the medieval pharmacy[/44]. Doctors believed that the 'hot' and 'dry' nature of cloves could balance the 'cold' humors of the body, making them a primary treatment for everything from toothaches to the Black Death. This medicinal value added another layer of demand to an already frantic market. ||(45)|| [45]As production increased and new growing regions were established in the Caribbean and Africa, the rarity that had defined the spice trade for millennia vanished[/45]. What was once a symbol of extreme wealth and status became a staple of the common kitchen. The era of the 'Spice Wars' was effectively over. ||(46)|| [46]We rarely stop to consider that the contents of our spice rack represent centuries of exploration, corporate espionage, and the birth of global capitalism[/46]. Every sprinkle of pepper is a reminder of a time when the world was willing to risk everything for a taste of the exotic.

paragraphs.A: Indeed, for the [41] ancient Greeks and Romans [/41], spices were shrouded in legend. They knew that these goods arrived from the Far East, but the exact geographical origins remained a closely guarded secret, known only to the traders who braved the long journey through the Silk Road.
paragraphs.B: However, [42] these tall tales could only last so long [/42]. As European maritime technology improved in the 15th century, the desire to find the source of these riches became a matter of national priority for the Portuguese and Spanish crowns, leading to the first great voyages of exploration.
paragraphs.C: This [43] European entry into the market [/43] led to a century of blood-soaked rivalry. The English, French, and Dutch all established their own trading companies, each vying for exclusive rights to the lucrative islands of the Moluccas, often resulting in naval battles and the displacement of indigenous populations.
paragraphs.D: Such [44] draconian measures [/44] were not limited to destruction; they also included the forced labor of local inhabitants. The wealth generated by these monopolies funded the Golden Age of the Netherlands, building the iconic canals of Amsterdam and financing the works of masters like Rembrandt.
paragraphs.E: Despite this [45] perceived healing power [/45], the primary driver of the trade remained the culinary prestige of spices. A host who could serve a dish heavily seasoned with ginger and saffron was signaling his immense social standing to his guests, regardless of the actual flavor of the food.
paragraphs.F: The [46] eventual democratization of flavor [/46] occurred during the Industrial Revolution. Faster transportation and better storage techniques meant that spices could be imported in bulk, finally bringing down the costs to a level that the average working-class family could afford.
paragraphs.G: Many [47] modern chefs [/47] are now attempting to rediscover the 'pure' taste of these ancient varieties. They argue that mass-produced spices lack the complexity and oils of those grown in traditional volcanic soils, leading to a resurgence in boutique, ethically-sourced spice trading.

questions.explanation: A refers to the 'ancient Greeks and Romans' and the 'closely guarded secret' of spice origins, which leads into the 'marketing strategy' and 'myths' described in the next paragraph.
B refers to 'these tall tales' (the myths about giant birds) and explains how improved technology ended the merchants' monopoly, leading to the voyages mentioned after the gap.
C describes the 'European entry into the market' following da Gama's voyage and mentions the 'intense colonial competition' that the main text continues to discuss.
D refers to the 'draconian measures' (burning plantations) described just before the gap and connects the wealth generated to the Dutch Golden Age.
E links back to the 'perceived healing power' (medicinal use) mentioned before the gap while introducing the 'culinary prestige' that the text focuses on next.
F explains the 'democratization of flavor' (falling prices), which explains why spices are no longer 'symbols of extreme wealth' as noted in the final section.
